title: express-rate-limit with max: 0 — the limiter is disabled, so there is no brute-force / abuse throttle

params.passDetail: express-rate-limit sets a positive max — requests beyond the threshold in the window are throttled.
params.failDetail: express-rate-limit is configured with max: 0, which disables the limit entirely (0 means no cap). The middleware is mounted and looks like it protects the route, but it never throttles anything — login, OTP, and password-reset endpoints can be brute-forced without limit. Set max to a real per-window cap (e.g. 5 for auth endpoints).
params.absentCallDetail: No rateLimit() call in this scope; this rule does not apply here.
params.absentArgDetail: rateLimit() does not set max as a literal; verify the resolved cap is a positive number, not 0.

provenance.note: max: 0 reads like 'no configured limit yet' but express-rate-limit treats 0 as unlimited, so the limiter is a no-op. Mounted on an auth route it gives a false sense of protection while allowing unbounded brute force.
